items.title: Sudan's war enters a fourth year with the world's largest humanitarian crisis still underfunded
items.body: WHO reports 34 million people needing aid in Sudan, 21 million lacking health services, repeated attacks on health care, and disease outbreaks across multiple states.
items.summary: Sudan remains the largest humanitarian and health crisis in the world.

items.title: Myanmar 2026 humanitarian plan warns 16 million people need life-saving assistance
items.body: The UN in Myanmar says conflict, disaster, displacement, and economic collapse continue to compound needs. Humanitarians warn that Myanmar remains dire and under-funded.
items.summary: Myanmar needs are rising while funding is narrowing.

items.title: Haiti access crisis keeps aid, food, health, water, and education under severe pressure
items.body: UN reporting says armed groups control large parts of Port-au-Prince, more than 1.4 million people have fled their homes, and six million people need some form of humanitarian assistance in 2026.
items.summary: Haiti remains overlooked and badly underfunded.

items.title: Global Report on Food Crises 2026: acute hunger remains entrenched across protracted crises
items.body: The 2026 GRFC reports severe food insecurity concentrated in protracted crisis contexts, with conflict as the main driver and malnutrition still critical.
items.summary: Food-system data belongs on the front page.

items.title: WFP 2026 outlook: acute hunger remains double pre-pandemic levels
items.body: WFP estimates 318 million people face acute hunger in 2026, with 41 million at Emergency levels or worse. Conflict, climate shocks, and funding shortfalls keep the pressure high.
items.summary: Conflict and climate shocks compound hunger.

items.title: #WHO Responds to #Hantavirus Outbreak
items.body: The World Health Organization is supporting the response to a hantavirus outbreak aboard a passenger ship after its arrival in Tenerife, Canary Islands. Passengers and crew are disembarking under health monitoring measures, with specially arranged flights taking them home as WHO teams continue investigations and advise governments on follow-up procedures.

Passengers and crew are disembarking under health monitoring measures, with specially arranged flights taking them home as WHO teams continue investigations and advise governments on follow-up procedures.

items.title: WHO Director-General Dr Tedros updates on hantavirus at press conference
items.body: WHO Director-General Dr Tedros participated in a press conference on hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel on the 8th of May 2026. WHO will continue to work with all relevant governments and partners to provide care for those who are affected, protect the safety and dignity of passengers, and prevent onward spread of the virus. items.summary: The World Health Organization (WHO) has validated Australia for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem, marking a significant milestone in the health of indigenous peoples and in global efforts to combat neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). Trachoma, the world’s leading infectious cause of blindness, no longer represents a public health problem in the country.

• The Big Catch-Up, launched during World Immunization Week 2023, has delivered over 100 million vaccine doses to an estimated 18.3 million children across 36 countries
  • Around 12.3 million were “zero-dose children” who had not previously received any vaccines and 15 million who had never received a measles vaccine.
  • The initiative concluded in March 2026 and is on track to meet its target of catching up 21 million children – but agencies warn that many infants still miss out o
